reasoning: The `IsValidStrategy` definition is semantically wrong: it states `s g ∈ g` (the strategy returns an element IN the list of moves played so far), whereas a real game strategy must return an element NOT already chosen. Because `ConformsToStrategy` requires `g[i] = s(g.take i)` for odd i, and `IsValidStrategy` forces `s(g.take i) ∈ g.take i`, any valid game of length ≥ 2 would need `g[1] ∈ [g[0]]`, contradicting `g.Nodup`. Thus for every n ≥ 2 the universal quantifier over conforming valid games is vacuous, so `IsWinningFor n s` holds for any valid `s` trivially, and the LHS equals {n | 0 < n} for non-mathematical reasons. The informal statement asks a genuine combinatorial question about strategies in the real game (to force a chosen parity); the Lean statement, due to this bug, is a vacuously satisfied claim that doesn't encode "Bob has a winning strategy" in any real sense. The two statements are not the same mathematical claim even though both end up true.
